sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: ὦ τὴν ἐν ἄστροις οὐρανοῦ τέμνων ὁδὸν
sections.translation: O Sun-god , you who cut your path in heaven ’ s stars , mounted on a chariot inlaid with gold and whirling out your flame with swift horses , what an unfortunate beam you shed on Thebes , the day

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: ἀκτῖνʼ ἐφῆκας , Κάδμος ἡνίκʼ ἦλθε γῆν
sections.translation: that Cadmus left Phoenicia ’ s realm beside the sea and reached this land ! He married at that time Harmonia , the daughter of Cypris , and begot Polydorus from whom they say Labdacus was born , and Laius from him .

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ παῖς μὲν κλῄζομαι Μενοικέως ,
sections.translation: I am known as the daughter of Menoeceus , and Creon is my brother by the same mother . They call me Jocasta , for so my father named me , and I am married to Laius . Now when he was still childless after being married to me a long time in the palace ,

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: ἐλθὼν ἐρωτᾷ Φοῖβον ἐξαιτεῖ θʼ ἅμα
sections.translation: he went and questioned Phoebus , and asked for us both to have sons for the house . But the god said : Lord of Thebes famous for horses , do not sow a furrow of children against the will of the gods ; for if you beget a son , that child will kill you ,

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: καὶ πᾶς σὸς οἶκος βήσεται διʼ αἵματος .
sections.translation: and all your house shall wade through blood . But he , yielding to pleasure in a drunken fit , begot a child on me ; and afterwards , conscious of his sin and of the god ’ s warning , he gave the child to shepherds to expose

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: δίδωσι βουκόλοισιν ἐκθεῖναι βρέφος ,
sections.translation: in Hera ’ s meadow and the crag of Cithaeron , after piercing his ankles with iron spikes ; from which Hellas named him Oedipus . But Polybus ’ horsemen found him and took him home and laid him in the arms of their mistress .

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: ἔθηκαν . ἣ δὲ τὸν ἐμὸν ὠδίνων πόνον
sections.translation: So she suckled the child that I had borne and persuaded her husband she was its mother .

sections.label: Section 32
sections.original: ἤδη δὲ πυρσαῖς γένυσιν ἐξανδρούμενος
sections.translation: When my son had become a man , with tawny beard , either because he had guessed or learned it from another , he set out for the shrine of Phoebus , wanting to know for certain who his parents were ;

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: πρὸς δῶμα Φοίβου , Λάιός θʼ , οὑμὸς πόσις ,
sections.translation: and so did Laius , my husband , seeking to learn if the child he had exposed was dead . And the two of them met at the branching road of Phocis . And Laius ’ charioteer ordered him :

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: ὦ ξένε , τυράννοις ἐκποδὼν μεθίστασο .
sections.translation: Stranger , make way for the king ! But he walked on without a word , in his pride . The horses with their hoofs drew blood from the tendons of his feet . Then — why need I speak of matters outside these evils ?— son slew father , and taking his chariot

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: Πολύβῳ τροφεῖ δίδωσιν . ὡς δʼ ἐπεζάρει
sections.translation: gave it to Polybus , his foster-father . Now when the Sphinx was oppressing and ravaging our city , after my husband ’ s death , my brother Creon proclaimed my marriage : that he would marry me to anyone who should guess the riddle of the crafty maiden . It happened somehow

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: μούσας ἐμὸς παῖς Οἰδίπους Σφιγγὸς μαθών ·
sections.translation: that my son , Oedipus , guessed the Sphinx ’ s song ; and so he became king of this land and received the scepter of this land as his prize . He married his mother in ignorance , luckless wretch ! nor did his mother know that she was sleeping with her son .

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: τίκτω δὲ παῖδας παιδὶ δύο μὲν ἄρσενας ,
sections.translation: I bore my son two sons , Eteocles and the hero Polyneices , and two daughters ; the one her father called Ismene ; the other , which was the elder , I named Antigone . Now when Oedipus , who endured so much ,

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: ὁ πάντʼ ἀνατλὰς Οἰδίπους παθήματα
sections.translation: learned that he was married to his mother , he inflicted a dreadful slaughter upon his eyes , making the pupils bloody with a golden brooch . But when my sons grew to bearded men , they hid their father behind bars , so that his misfortune ,

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: γένοιτο πολλῶν δεομένη σοφισμάτων ·
sections.translation: needing as it did much skill to hide it , might be forgotten . He is still living in the house . Afflicted by his fate , he makes the most unholy curses against his sons , praying that they may divide this house with a sharp sword .

sections.label: Section 69
sections.original: τὼ δʼ ἐς φόβον πεσόντε , μὴ τελεσφόρους
sections.translation: So they were afraid

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: εὐχὰς θεοὶ κραίνωσιν οἰκούντων ὁμοῦ ,
sections.translation: that the gods might fulfil his prayers if they dwell together ; and they made an agreement , that Polyneices , the younger , should first leave the land in voluntary exile , while Eteocles should stay and hold the scepter , and then change places yearly . But as soon as Eteocles was seated on the bench of power ,

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: καθέζετʼ ἀρχῆς , οὐ μεθίσταται θρόνων ,
sections.translation: he did not leave the throne , but drove Polyneices into exile from this land . So Polyneices went to Argos and married into the family of Adrastus , and having collected a numerous force of Argives is leading them here ; and he has come against these very walls of seven gates ,

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: πατρῷʼ ἀπαιτεῖ σκῆπτρα καὶ μέρη χθονός .
sections.translation: demanding the scepter of his father and his share of the land . Now I , to end their strife , have persuaded one son to meet the other under truce , before seizing arms ; and the messenger I sent tells me that he will come . O Zeus , dwelling in the bright folds of heaven ,

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: Ζεῦ , σῶσον ἡμᾶς , δὸς δὲ σύμβασιν τέκνοις .
sections.translation: save us , and reconcile my sons ! For you , if you are really wise , must not allow the same mortal to be forever wretched . Exit Jocasta .

sections.label: Section 88
sections.original: ὦ κλεινὸν οἴκοις Ἀντιγόνη θάλος πατρί ,
sections.translation: From the roof . Antigone , famous child in your father ’ s house , although your mother allowed you at your entreaty to leave your maiden chamber

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: μεθῆκε μελάθρων ἐς διῆρες ἔσχατον
sections.translation: for the topmost story of the house , to see the Argive army , wait , so that I may first investigate the path , whether there be any of the citizens visible on the road , and reproach , a slight matter to a slave like me , should come

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: σοὶ δʼ ὡς ἀνάσσῃ · πάντα δʼ ἐξειδὼς φράσω
sections.translation: to you , my royal mistress ; and when I have examined everything , I will tell you what I saw and heard from the Argives , when I carried the terms of the truce from here to Polyneices and back from him again .

sections.label: Section 99
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὔτις ἀστῶν τοῖσδε χρίμπτεται δόμοις ,
sections.translation: No , there is no citizen near the house ,

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: κέδρου παλαιὰν κλίμακʼ ἐκπέρα ποδί ·
sections.translation: so mount the ancient cedar steps , and view the plains ; beside Ismenus ’ streams and the fountain of Dirce see the great army of the enemy .

sections.label: Section 103
sections.original: ὄρεγέ νυν ὄρεγε γεραιὰν νέᾳ
sections.translation: Stretch out your hand to me from the stairs now , stretch it out , the hand of age to youth ,

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: ποδὸς ἴχνος ἐπαντέλλων .
sections.translation: helping me to rise .

sections.label: Section 106
sections.original: ἰδοὺ ξύναψον , παρθένʼ · ἐς καιρὸν δʼ ἔβης ·
sections.translation: There ! clasp it , maiden ; you have come at the right time ; for Pelasgia ’ s army is just upon the move , and they are separating the companies .

sections.label: Section 109
sections.original: ἰὼ πότνια παῖ
sections.translation: O Lady Hecate ,

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: Λατοῦς Ἑκάτα , κατάχαλκον ἅπαν
sections.translation: child of Leto ! The plain is one lightning-flash of bronze .

sections.label: Section 112
sections.original: οὐ γάρ τι φαύλως ἦλθε Πολυνείκης χθόνα ,
sections.translation: Ah ! this is no ordinary home-coming of Polyneices , with the clash of many horses , many arms .

sections.label: Section 114
sections.original: ἆρα πύλαι κλῄθροις — χαλκόδετʼ ἆρʼ ἔμβολα
sections.translation: Are the gates barred , and the brazen bolts

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: λαϊνέοισιν Ἀμφίονος ὀργάνοις
sections.translation: fitted into Amphion ’ s walls of stone ?

sections.label: Section 117
sections.original: θάρσει · τά γʼ ἔνδον ἀσφαλῶς ἔχει πόλις .
sections.translation: Never fear ! All is safe within the town . But see the first one , if you want to know him .

sections.label: Section 119
sections.original: τίς οὗτος ὁ λευκολόφας ,
sections.translation: Who is that one with the white crest ,

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: πρόπαρ ὃς ἁγεῖται στρατοῦ πάγχαλκον ἀσπίδʼ
sections.translation: who marches before the army , lightly bearing on his arm a shield all of bronze ?

sections.label: Section 123
sections.original: λοχαγός , ὦ δέσποινα .
sections.translation: A captain , mistress .

sections.label: Section 123b
sections.original: τίς , πόθεν γεγώς ;
sections.translation: Who is he ? Who is his family ? Tell me his name , old man .

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: οὗτος Μυκηναῖος μὲν αὐδᾶται γένος ,
sections.translation: He claims to be Mycenaean ; by Lerna ’ s streams he dwells , the lord Hippomedon .

sections.label: Section 127
sections.original: ἒ ἒ ὡς γαῦρος , ὡς φοβερὸς εἰσιδεῖν ,
sections.translation: Ah , ah ! How proud , how fearful to see , like an earth-born giant , with stars engraved on his shield , not resembling

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: ἁμερίῳ γέννᾳ .
sections.translation: mortal race .

sections.label: Section 131
sections.original: τὸν δʼ ἐξαμείβοντʼ οὐχ ὁρᾷς Δίρκης ὕδωρ ;
sections.translation: Do you see the one crossing Dirce ’ s stream ?

sections.label: Section 132
sections.original: ἄλλος ἄλλος ὅδε τευχέων τρόπος .
sections.translation: His armor is quite different . Who is that ?

sections.label: Section 133b
sections.original: παῖς μὲν Οἰνέως ἔφυ
sections.translation: Tydeus , the son of Oeneus , Aetolian battle-spirit in his breast .

sections.label: Section 135
sections.original: οὗτος ὁ τᾶς Πολυνείκεος ,
sections.translation: Is this the one , old man , who married a sister of Polyneices ’ wife ? What a foreign look his armor has , half-barbarian !

sections.label: Section 139
sections.original: σακεσφόροι γὰρ πάντες Αἰτωλοί , τέκνον ,
sections.translation: Yes , my child ; all Aetolians carry shields ,

sections.label: Section 140
sections.original: λόγχαις τʼ ἀκοντιστῆρες εὐστοχώτατοι .
sections.translation: and are most unerring marksmen with their darts .

sections.label: Section 141
sections.original: σὺ δʼ , ὦ γέρον , πῶς αἰσθάνῃ σαφῶς τάδε ;
sections.translation: How do you know them so clearly , old man ?

sections.label: Section 142
sections.original: σημεῖʼ ἰδὼν τότʼ ἀσπίδων ἐγνώρισα ,
sections.translation: I saw and learned the devices on their shields before , when I went with the terms of the truce to your brother , since I looked closely at them , I know the armed men .

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: τίς δʼ οὗτος ἀμφὶ μνῆμα τὸ Ζήθου περᾷ
sections.translation: Who is that youth passing by the tomb of Zethus , with long flowing hair , fierce to see ? Is he a captain ? For an armed crowd follows at his heels .

sections.label: Section 150
sections.original: ὅδʼ ἐστὶ Παρθενοπαῖος , Ἀταλάντης γόνος .
sections.translation: That is Parthenopaeus , Atalanta ’ s son .

sections.label: Section 151
sections.original: ἀλλά νιν ἁ κατʼ ὄρη μετὰ ματέρος
sections.translation: May Artemis , who rushes over the hills with his mother , lay him low with an arrow , for coming against my city to sack it !

sections.label: Section 154
sections.original: εἴη τάδʼ , ὦ παῖ . σὺν δίκῃ δʼ ἥκουσι γῆν ·
sections.translation: May it be so , my child ; but they have come here with justice ,

sections.label: Section 155
sections.original: ὃ καὶ δέδοικα μὴ σκοπῶσʼ ὀρθῶς θεοί .
sections.translation: and my fear is that the gods will take the rightful view .

sections.label: Section 156
sections.original: ποῦ δʼ ὃς ἐμοὶ μιᾶς ἐγένετʼ ἐκ ματρὸς
sections.translation: Where is the one who was born of the same mother as I was , by a painful destiny ? Oh ! tell me , old friend , where Polyneices is .

sections.label: Section 159
sections.original: ἐκεῖνος ἑπτὰ παρθένων τάφου πέλας
sections.translation: He is standing by Adrastus ,

sections.label: Section 160
sections.original: Νιόβης Ἀδράστῳ πλησίον παραστατεῖ .
sections.translation: near the tomb of Niobe ’ s seven unwed daughters . Do you see him ?

sections.label: Section 161b
sections.original: ὁρῶ δῆτʼ οὐ σαφῶς , ὁρῶ δέ πως
sections.translation: I see him , yes ! but not clearly ; I see the outline of his form , the likeness of his breast . Would I could speed through the sky , swift as a cloud before the wind ,

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: πρὸς ἐμὸν ὁμογενέτορα , περὶ δʼ ὠλένας
sections.translation: towards my own dear brother , and throw my arms about my darling ’ s neck , so long , poor boy ! an exile . How distinguished he is with his golden weapons , old man , flashing like the morning rays !

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: ἥξει δόμους τούσδʼ , ὥστε σʼ ἐμπλῆσαι χαρᾶς ,
sections.translation: He will come to this house , under truce , to fill your heart with joy .
